name: Neurogenetics
html:
Malformations of Brain Development

Developmental Timing Framework

Gestational ageDominant processKey CNS eventsExample disorders
3–4 weeksPrimary neurulationNeural tube and crest; closure of rostral and caudal neuroporesAnencephaly, encephalocele, myelomeningocele, Chiari II deformation
4–6 weeksTelencephalizationForebrain/face development; cerebral vesicles; optic/olfactory placodes; early posterior fossa formationHoloprosencephaly, Dandy–Walker spectrum, craniosynostosis
6–16 weeksNeuronal/glial proliferationProliferation in ventricular/subventricular zones; early neuroblast differentiation; apoptosisPrimary microcephaly, megalencephaly
12–24 weeksNeuronal migrationFormation of gyri; GM/WM differentiation; corpus callosum formationLissencephaly, pachygyria, polymicrogyria, schizencephaly, heterotopia, agenesis of corpus callosum
24 wk–postnatalCortical organizationLate migration; cortical organization; synaptogenesisFocal/mild cortical dysplasias
24 wk–2 yearsMyelinationMyelination; vulnerability to destructive processesHypomyelination, leukodystrophies, post‑injury WM loss

Selected Brain Malformations

Agenesis of the Corpus Callosum (ACC)

Multiple genetic and acquired causes; isolated or syndromic.

Clinical spectrum: normal with subtle interhemispheric deficits → global developmental delay/intellectual disability and epilepsy.

Aicardi syndrome (classically in girls): ACC with polymicrogyria, heterotopia, unlayered cortex; chorioretinal lacunae; vertebral/rib anomalies; infantile spasms.

Holoprosencephaly

Incomplete forebrain separation (alobar, semilobar, lobar).

Seizures, significant intellectual disability, endocrine dysfunction, facial midline anomalies, other midline defects.

Often due to SHH and related developmental genes or chromosomal abnormalities.

Optic Nerve Hypoplasia / Septo‑Optic Dysplasia

Small optic nerves/chiasm, absent septum pellucidum ± hypothalamic/pituitary anomalies and other dysplasias.

Visual impairment, pituitary hormone deficiencies, seizures if associated cortical malformations.

Requires coordinated ophthalmology, endocrine, and neurology care.

Hemimegalencephaly

Overgrowth and dysplasia of a cerebral hemisphere; often mTOR‑pathway or mosaic overgrowth.

Macrocephaly or asymmetric head, contralateral hemiparesis, early refractory focal epilepsy.

Hemispherectomy/hemispherotomy often required for epileptic encephalopathy.

Lissencephaly and Related Cortical Malformations

Type 1 (classic): agyria/pachygyria with thickened cortex ± band heterotopia.

  • LIS1/PAFAH1B1: posterior‑predominant.
  • DCX (X‑linked): anterior‑predominant in males; subcortical band heterotopia in females.

Type 2 (cobblestone): cobblestone cortex in dystroglycanopathies (Walker–Warburg, Fukuyama, muscle–eye–brain).

Usually severe developmental impairment, early epilepsy, feeding/respiratory difficulties.

Skull and Posterior Fossa

Fontanelle Closure

Posterior: usually 1–2 months (up to 6 months can be normal).

Anterior: usually 9–18 months.

Craniosynostosis

Premature fusion of cranial sutures → abnormal head shape ± raised ICP.

Syndromic forms (often FGFR‑pathway):

  • Apert: bicoronal synostosis, brachycephaly, syndactyly; may have ACC and other anomalies.
  • Crouzon: craniosynostosis with midface hypoplasia; risk of elevated ICP.
  • Carpenter: multi‑suture synostosis with poly/syndactyly; possible hypogonadism, obesity, brain anomalies.

Chiari Deformation

Chiari I deformation

5 mm caudal displacement of cerebellar tonsils through foramen magnum.

Often incidental; symptomatic cases: Valsalva‑triggered occipital headache, neck pain, ataxia, cranial neuropathies, syringomyelia.

Chiari II ("Arnold–Chiari")

Chiari I plus myelomeningocele and small posterior fossa; beaked tectum, towering cerebellum, hydrocephalus.

Chiari III

Chiari II plus occipital or high cervical encephalocele.

Dandy–Walker Spectrum

Classic Dandy–Walker malformation:

  • Cystic dilation of 4th ventricle.
  • Hypoplastic/elevated vermis.
  • Enlarged posterior fossa with high tentorium/torcula.
  • Hydrocephalus is common.

"Dandy–Walker variant": variably hypoplastic vermis/posterior fossa anomalies.

Often associated with ACC, other cortical malformations, Walker–Warburg, Joubert and related pontocerebellar hypoplasias.

Prognosis largely determined by associated anomalies.

Neurocutaneous Disorders

NF1 Diagnostic Criteria

Neurofibromatosis Type 1 — need ≥2 of the following criteria
Parent with NF1
Six or more café‑au‑lait macules (>5 mm prepubertal; >15 mm postpubertal)
Axillary or inguinal freckling
Two or more neurofibromas of any type OR one plexiform neurofibroma
Optic pathway glioma
Two or more Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas)
Distinctive osseous lesion (e.g., sphenoid wing dysplasia, cortical thinning ± pseudarthrosis)
Genetics: autosomal dominant; NF1, 17q.

Neurofibromatosis Type 2 (NF2)

Genetics: AD, NF2 (22q).

Bilateral vestibular schwannomas; meningiomas, spinal ependymomas, early cataracts.

Tuberous Sclerosis Complex (TSC)

Genetics: AD; TSC1 (hamartin) and TSC2 (tuberin).

CNS: cortical tubers, radial migration lines, subependymal nodules, SEGAs; epilepsy (incl. infantile spasms), ID, ASD.

Systemic: renal angiomyolipomas/cysts, cardiac rhabdomyomas, pulmonary LAM, characteristic skin lesions.

Screening: periodic brain MRI, renal imaging, echo/ECG, chest imaging in at‑risk adolescents/adults.

Treatment: vigabatrin for spasms; ASMs and epilepsy surgery; mTOR inhibitors for SEGAs and some seizure phenotypes.

TSC Diagnostic Criteria

Level of certaintyDefinition
Definite TSC2 major features or 1 major feature + ≥2 minor features
Probable TSC1 major feature + 1 minor feature
Possible TSC1 major feature or ≥2 minor features
Major featuresMinor features
Facial angiofibromas or forehead plaqueMultiple randomly distributed pits in dental enamel
Hypomelanotic macules (≥3)Hamartomatous rectal polyps
Shagreen patchBone cysts
Ungual or periungual fibromaCerebral white‑matter migration lines
Multiple retinal nodular hamartomasGingival fibromas
Cortical tuberNon‑renal hamartoma
Subependymal noduleRetinal achromic patch
Subependymal giant cell astrocytoma"Confetti" skin lesions
Cardiac rhabdomyoma, single or multipleMultiple renal cysts
Lymphangiomyomatosis
Renal angiomyolipoma

Sturge–Weber Syndrome

Somatic activating GNAQ mutation (sporadic).

Unilateral facial port‑wine stain (V1 distribution); leptomeningeal angioma; glaucoma/buphthalmos.

Seizures, hemiparesis, visual field deficits, cognitive impairment.

"Tram‑track" cortical calcifications on CT.

Other Neurocutaneous Reminders

  • Incontinentia pigmenti – vesicular rash → streaky hyperpigmentation; seizures and developmental issues.
  • Hypomelanosis of Ito – whorled/linear hypopigmentation; often seizures and ID.
  • Ataxia telangiectasia – progressive ataxia, telangiectasias, immunodeficiency, elevated AFP, malignancy risk.
  • Gorlin syndrome – basal cell carcinomas, jaw cysts, desmoplastic medulloblastoma.

Neuro‑Metabolic Disorders: General Approach

Conceptual Split

Small‑molecule / intoxication type – aminoacidopathies, organic acidemias, urea cycle defects, some energy disorders; acute or intermittent crises with acidosis, hyperammonemia, hypoglycemia.

Large‑molecule / organelle – lysosomal, peroxisomal, many glycogen and glycosylation disorders; chronic progressive neurodegeneration with visceromegaly and dysmorphism.

Clinical Questions

  • Multi‑organ involvement?
  • Gray vs white‑matter predominance on MRI?
  • Macrocephaly vs microcephaly?
  • Static vs regressive vs episodic?

Basic Metabolic Work‑up

First‑Tier (Screen)

Blood:

  • Ammonia, lactate.
  • Plasma amino acids.
  • Acylcarnitine profile.
  • Total homocysteine.
  • Copper, ceruloplasmin.

Urine:

  • Organic acids.
  • Purines/pyrimidines.
  • Creatine metabolites.
  • Oligosaccharides.
  • Glycosaminoglycans.

Second‑Tier (Targeted)

  • VLCFAs, plasmalogens.
  • Cholesterol and 7‑dehydrocholesterol.
  • Pipecolic acid / AASA.
  • CSF amino acids, neurotransmitters, lactate/pyruvate, 5‑MTHF, glucose.
  • Lysosomal enzyme panels; sterol/bile‑acid panels.
  • Exome/genome or targeted sequencing.

Treatable‑First Disorders

Use early in regression, refractory epilepsy, crises, or evolving movement disorders.

DisorderKey cluesUrgent testsInitial treatment
GLUT1 deficiencyEarly seizures, microcephaly, exercise‑induced dyskinesiaCSF/serum glucose ratio; SLC2A1Ketogenic diet
Neurotransmitter synthesis defectsOculogyric crises, parkinsonism/dystonia, autonomic featuresCSF monoamines, BH4, neopterinLevodopa, 5‑HTP, BH4, folinic acid, etc.
Urea cycle defectsHyperammonemia, respiratory alkalosis, encephalopathyAmmonia, plasma AAs, urine orotic acidScavengers, dialysis, protein restriction
Biotinidase / multiple carboxylase deficiencySeizures, hypotonia, dermatitis, alopeciaBiotinidase activity, organic acidsHigh‑dose biotin
CTXAtaxia, early cataracts, tendon xanthomasCholestanol, bile acidsChenodeoxycholic acid
X‑ALDSchool‑age boy, posterior leukodystrophy, adrenal failureVLCFA, ABCD1Early HSCT; treat adrenal insufficiency
Early MLD / KrabbeRegression, spasticity, neuropathyArylsulfatase A, GALC, geneticsHSCT/gene therapy if early

Mitochondrial Disorders

Maternal (mtDNA), autosomal recessive, or autosomal dominant inheritance.

Multisystem involvement; neurologic plus myopathy, cardiomyopathy/arrhythmia, endocrine, GI, renal disease.

Triggers: fever, fasting, infection, some medications (e.g., valproate in suspected POLG disease).

Testing: lactate ± pyruvate, CK, MRI, mtDNA/nuclear panels, RC chain analysis ± biopsy.

Management: avoid mitochondrial toxins; supportive care; CoQ10 or targeted therapies in specific deficiencies.

Recognizable Mitochondrial Syndromes

Syndrome and featuresGenetics
Leigh syndrome – subacute encephalopathy with symmetric brainstem/basal ganglia lesionsAR, mtDNA, or X‑linked
Pearson syndrome – sideroblastic anemia, pancytopenia, exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, renal tubulopathymtDNA
MERRF – myoclonic epilepsy with ragged‑red fibersmtDNA
NARP – neurogenic weakness, ataxia, retinitis pigmentosamtDNA
MELAS – mitochondrial encephalopathy, lactic acidosis, stroke‑like episodesmtDNA
Alpers syndrome – encephalopathy, seizures, hepatic dysfunction (often POLG)Nuclear (AR/AD)
MNGIE – mitochondrial neurogastrointestinal encephalopathyAR
Kearns–Sayre syndrome – CPEO, pigmentary retinopathy, high CSF protein, ataxia, conduction defects (<20 yrs)mtDNA deletions
MIDD – maternally inherited diabetes and deafnessmtDNA
SANDO – sensory ataxia, neuropathy, dysarthria, ophthalmoplegiaAD (nuclear)

Leukodystrophies / Leukoencephalopathies – Pattern Table

DiseaseMRI patternKey testClinical clues
X‑ALDPosterior WM → anterior; enhancing edgeVLCFASchool‑age boy, behavioral/visual decline, adrenal failure
MLDPeriventricular + cerebellar WM; tigroidArylsulfatase ARegression, spasticity, neuropathy
KrabbeCSTs, cerebellar WM, thick nerve rootsGALC enzymeExtreme irritability, early neuropathy, opisthotonos
CanavanDiffuse WM with U‑fiber involvement; high NAAUrine/MRS NAAMacrocephaly, hypotonia, regression
AlexanderFrontal WM, basal ganglia/brainstem; enhancementGFAPMacrocephaly, seizures, regression
Vanishing white matterRarefied/cystic WM, CSF‑like signalEIF2B genesStress‑related deterioration, ataxia, seizures

When to Test Now vs When to Defer

Test now for regression, evolving movement disorder, refractory epilepsy, progressive MRI changes, metabolic crises, strong family history, or a treatable‑first phenotype.

Stage/defer only for clearly static encephalopathy with well‑documented perinatal insult and no red flags, or mild stable delay—though baseline genetic testing is still encouraged.

Developmental Delay ± Epilepsy – Genetic and Metabolic Work‑up

Step 1 – Clinical Triage

  • History: prenatal/perinatal events; regression vs static course; seizure types; family history.
  • Exam: distinctive features (dysmorphism, neurocutaneous signs), head size, organomegaly, movement disorder.
  • MRI brain when focal deficits, abnormal head growth, refractory epilepsy, or concerning exam.

Step 2 – Broad Genetic Testing

If the child meets criteria for global developmental delay (≥2 domains, moderate/severe) with any combination of ID, ASD, epilepsy, abnormal exam, or regression/evolving picture, or is not responding to standard treatment:

  • Order trio WES/WGS as the primary test.
  • Add targeted or syndrome‑specific panels only when clearly indicated.

Step 3 – Additional Studies and Follow‑up

  • Chromosomal microarray (if not already done).
  • Fragile X testing (especially males or suggestive family history).
  • Metabolic testing that may have been missed, guided by phenotype.
  • Consider EEG to evaluate for ESES or other epileptic encephalopathy patterns.
  • Consider repeat MRI if concern for evolving leukodystrophy or basal‑ganglia changes.
  • Refer to a Neurogenetics clinic when expedited, comprehensive evaluation or VUS resolution is needed.

Genetic Counseling, Broad Testing, and GINA

Why Genetic Counseling Is Needed

Broad testing (WES/WGS, large panels) should be ordered with pre‑test counseling by a genetics professional.

Counseling covers possible outcomes (positive, negative, VUS), test limitations, family implications, and the chance of unexpected findings (secondary findings, consanguinity, non‑paternity).

Result Types

  • Positive – pathogenic/likely pathogenic variant explaining the phenotype; guides management and family testing.
  • Negative – no causative variant found; a genetic cause may still exist.
  • Variant of uncertain significance (VUS) – uncertain; should not usually change management. Most reclassified VUS move downward toward benign/likely benign rather than toward pathogenic.

GINA – Key Points for Families (U.S.)

  • GINA limits how health insurers and most employers can use genetic information.
  • Health insurers cannot use genetic test results to set eligibility or premiums or require testing.
  • Employers with ≥15 employees cannot use genetic information in hiring, firing, or promotion decisions.
  • GINA does not cover life, disability, or long‑term‑care insurance, and does not apply to very small employers; families should understand these limits when deciding about testing.
